coredata.dc:title: A wide range photoluminescence intensity-based temperature sensor developed with BN quantum dots and the photoluminescence mechanism
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Qun
Zheng, Yapeng
Hou, Xinmei
Yang, Tao
Liang, Tongxiang
Zheng, Jinju
coredata.dc:description: Boron nitride nanosheets (BNNSs), as one of the typical two dimensional (2D) few-layered nanomaterials with a structure similar to graphite, have been widely explored and developed due to their unique properties. Recently the temperature sensor based on BN stimulates people’s research interest due to its chemical stability especially at high temperature. In this work, an improved top-down approach was adopted to prepare BN quantum dots (BNQDs) using ultrathin BNNSs as precursors. Strong blue and blue-green photoluminescence (PL) are observed for BNNSs and BNQDs with quantum yields (QYs) of 15.4% and 10%, respectively. Furthermore, for the first time, the temperature-dependent PL of the BNQDs was also researched. Based on this, the activation energy for thermal quenching/population and variation of energy gap with temperature were investigated to understand the PL origin and mechanism. The results show that the broad PL band of BNQDs is related to the strong electron−electron interactions and weak electron−phonon interactions. Meanwhile, the good correspondence between the PL intensity and temperature suggests that BNQDs can be exploited for temperature sensors. The photostability and storage stability as well as excellent reversibility ensure the BNQDs especially suitable for stable and wide-range PL intensity-based temperature sensors.
coredata.dcterms:subject: BN quantum dot
Photoluminescence
Temperature-dependence
Sensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Optical properties and PL of BNNSs
Optical properties and PL of BNQDs
Temperature-dependent PL and application as the temperature sensor

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The schematic of the preparation of BNQDs.
XRD and FTIR of the BNNSs synthesized at different temperature.
SEM of the BNNSs synthesized at different temperature. (a) 600 °C, (b) 700 °C (c) 800 °C and (d) 900 °C.
(a) XRD patterns, (b) Raman, (c) FTIR of BNNSs and BNQDs. XPS spectra of B1s (d), N 1s (e) and (f) C1s of BNQDs.

(a) SEM and (b) TEM image of the BNNSs sample, (c) AFM image and (d) the height profile performed along the white line A–B of BNNSs after sonication treatment, (e) TEM image and (f) size distributions of BNQDs.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectrum of BNNSs after sonication treatment, and the inset of photographs taken under room light and UV light (365 nm), (b) PL spectra of BNNSs excited with different wavelengths from 300 nm to 400 nm.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectrum, (b) PL (EM) and PLE (EX) spectra, (c) the photographs taken under room light and UV light (365 nm), (d) PL spectra excited with progressively increasing wavelengths from 310 to 480 nm, (e) PL decay curve for the as-prepared BNQDs, and (f) time dependence of PL intensities of the as-prepared BNQDs under continuous UV (320 nm) irradiation.

(a) Temperature-dependent PL of BNQDs, (b) the plot of PL intensity variation with temperature, (c) Arrhenius plot for BNQDs and the red line is the fit curve, temperature-dependent FWHM of the PL peaks (d) and peak energy (e) for BNQDs, (f) sensitivity and relative sensitivity of sensors based on BNQDs with the mean size of 4.6 nm, (g) the PL intensity upon cycling the temperature between 293 and 373 K for 10 times. The excitation wavelength is 394 nm and the PL intensity is monitored at 474 nm (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).
(a) TEM, (b) temperature-dependent PL, (c) the plot of PL intensity variation with temperature and (d) sensitivity and relative sensitivity of sensors based on BNQDs with the mean size of 17.73 nm.
Schematic of BNQDs setup as the temperature sensor.
Comparison of BNQDs with other QDs in literature for noncontact temperature sensors.

head.ce:title: A wide range photoluminescence intensity-based temperature sensor developed with BN quantum dots and the photoluminescence mechanism

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Collaborative Innovation Center of Steel Technology, University of Science and Technology Beijing, Beijing 100083, People’s Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Collaborative Innovation Center of Steel Technology
University of Science and Technology Beijing

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Collaborative Innovation Center of Steel Technology, University of Science and Technology Beijing, Beijing 100083, China.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Jiangxi University of Science and Technology, Ganzhou 341000, People’s Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering
Jiangxi University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Jiangxi University of Science and Technology, Ganzhou 341000, People’s Republic of China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Materials, Ningbo University of Technology, Ningbo City 315016, People’s Republic of China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Materials, Ningbo University of Technology, Ningbo City, 315016, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Boron nitride nanosheets (BNNSs), as one of the typical two dimensional (2D) few-layered nanomaterials with a structure similar to graphite, have been widely explored and developed due to their unique properties. Recently the temperature sensor based on BN stimulates people’s research interest due to its chemical stability especially at high temperature. In this work, an improved top-down approach was adopted to prepare BN quantum dots (BNQDs) using ultrathin BNNSs as precursors. Strong blue and blue-green photoluminescence (PL) are observed for BNNSs and BNQDs with quantum yields (QYs) of 15.4% and 10%, respectively. Furthermore, for the first time, the temperature-dependent PL of the BNQDs was also researched. Based on this, the activation energy for thermal quenching/population and variation of energy gap with temperature were investigated to understand the PL origin and mechanism. The results show that the broad PL band of BNQDs is related to the strong electron−electron interactions and weak electron−phonon interactions. Meanwhile, the good correspondence between the PL intensity and temperature suggests that BNQDs can be exploited for temperature sensors. The photostability and storage stability as well as excellent reversibility ensure the BNQDs especially suitable for stable and wide-range PL intensity-based temperature sensors.

ce:section.ce:para: The accurate measurement of temperature is of increasing importance in biochemical and physiological actions and processes [1,2]. Many promising approaches to local temperature sensors are being designed and explored, such as scanning probe microscopy, Raman spectroscopy, and fluorescence-based measurements [3,4]. Among them, fluorescence quantum dots (QDs) based temperature sensors have attracted most attention due to their fast response, high spatial resolution, and safety of remote handling [5,6]. The sensors are generally studied by measuring their PL intensity or lifetime change caused by temperature [7]. However, early QDs based temperature sensors generally suffer from poor long-term stability, limited temperature response range and even highly toxicity of the QDs [8,9]. Compared with traditional semiconductor QDs, the QDs derived from two dimensional (2D) layered BN-nanomaterials may be a promising candidate as temperature sensor due to their intrinsic low toxicity and good dispersibility [10].

Boron nitride nanosheets (BNNSs) are isostructural to graphite and have received considerable attention on account of their unique properties, such as wide band gap, high thermal conductivity and stability, and high resistance to oxidation and chemical inertness [11,12]. The outstanding properties render them applicable in various research fields including sensing, catalysis, lithium-sulfur batteries and optoelectronics [13–17]. To further broaden their application and even arise new exciting properties, BN quantum dots (BNQDs) have been synthesized by exfoliating and incising BNNSs via sonication-solvothermal methods [10,18,19]. These BNQDs show good optical properties and dispersibility due to the quantum confinement, edge effects, and defect centers, which motivates us to fully understand their photoluminescence (PL) mechanism and promote other applications. Compared with the widely studied BNNSs, the research on BNQDs is rare and is at its infant stage. Up to now, most of the references mainly focus on improving their PL quantum yield (QY) [10,18–20], while seldom studies are on their PL mechanism in detail. As we known, temperature-dependent PL spectrum is of paramount importance as it can be explored to evaluate the activation energy for thermal quenching/population and variation of energy gap with temperature, which could provide a further insight to understand the PL origin and mechanism [14,21,22]. However, it has never been reported for BNQDs. Furthermore, temperature-dependent fluorescent nanocrystals (NCs) have a wide application prospect in temperature sensors [23,24].

Herein, ultrathin BNNSs were firstly synthesized and then used to form BNQDs in DMF solvents with strong polarity by short time sonication and solvothermal process. The BNNSs and BNQDs emit strong blue light and blue-green light, respectively. The optical properties of BNNSs and BNQDs were compared in detail, indicating that the oxygenous functional groups on the surface of BNQDs have a strong effect on their PL characteristic. Besides, temperature-dependent PL and the PL decay curves were presented. Based on this, the PL mechanism and the application for wide-range temperature sensors of the BNQDs were also discussed.

The BNQDs were synthesized according to the procedures reported in literatures with some modifications [10,18,19]. The procedure involved two steps: (1) Synthesis of BNNSs using B2O3 and CO(NH2)2 as raw materials via nitridation in NH3. In a typical process, B2O3 and CO(NH2)2 with the molar ratio of 1:10 were added into 10 mL methanol under stirring to form a transparent solution. After 24 h, the white crystalline precursor was obtained. Then the precursor was ground and put into a quartz boat to heat to the required temperature (i.e 600, 700, 800 and 900 ℃) at a rate of 5℃/min in a chemical vapor deposition (CVD) tube furnace and then kept at that temperature for 3 h in NH3. When the furnace was cooled naturally to room temperature, the white ultrathin BNNSs were obtained. (2) Conversion of BNNSs into BNQDs via solvothermal process. As shown in Fig. 1 , the obtained few-layered BNNSs were dispersed in DMF by ultrasonic treatment at room temperature for 2 h. Then the above solution was put in a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave at 180 ℃ for 12 h and finally cooled naturally to room temperature. The light yellow suspension containing BNQDs was obtained for next study.

Multiple techniques including X-ray diffraction (XRD), scanning electron microscopy (SEM), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), Fourier transform infrared (FTIR), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and Raman were used to characterize the obtained samples. Besides, the optical properties and PL of the obtained samples were investigated using TU-1901 UV–vis spectrophotometers and FluoroMax-4 P spectrometers, respectively. The PL decay curves were analyzed using DAS6 software.

As is known, the precursors, i.e h-BN have large influence on the BNQDs properties, since the formation of QDs is directly from the exfoliation and disintegration of h-BN sheets. Thus, the effect factor of temperature on BNNSs is discussed. The crystal structure of the precursor BNNSs was firstly determined by XRD. As shown in Fig. 2 a, the diffraction peaks at different temperature have no obvious change, and a board peak at 24∼26° can be observed corresponding to the (002) facet of hexagonal BN (h-BN), which indicates the phase of h-BN has appeared at 600 ℃. Besides, all the peaks are obviously broadened when compared with those of crystalline h-BN, revealing these BNNSs exhibit nano-size or poor crystallinity [25,26]. The crystallinity of phases can be evaluated by full width at half maximum intensity (FWHM) of XRD [27–29]. The FTIR spectra of the samples are shown in Fig. 2b, two strong peaks at 1380 and 802 cm −
1 correspond to v(BN) and δ(BNB ) modes of h-BN, respectively [30]. However, the peaks at 1662 and 1086 cm −
1 can be only observed at 600 and 700 ℃, and they disappear with the increase of temperature, indicating the B2O3 is gradually nitrided to h-BN. The SEM images of the BNNSs synthesized at different temperature are shown in Fig. 3 . The morphology of h-BN transforms from cotton-like clusters (Fig. 3a and b), clusters and sheets mixture (Fig. 3c) to finally sheets (Fig. 3d) with temperature increasing. Considering the sheets prefer to be exfoliated and incised into BNQDs with small lateral sizes, BNNSs synthesized at 900 ℃ are selected for the following experiments. BNNSs can be prepared successfully by controlling the temperature.

Fig. 4 shows the comparison of characterization between BNNSs and BNQDs. As shown in Fig. 4a, after the solvothermal treatment, a 2.21° shift toward lower angles is clearly observed, which may be related to the intercalation of the solvent molecules in the inter-layer space due to the strong adsorption capacity of h-BN via nucleophilic attack of O to B in terms of the Lewis acid-base interaction [11,31]. Raman spectrum was used to study the crystalline structure of the samples. In Fig. 4b, the BNNSs exhibit an intrinsic E2g phonon mode at 1377 cm−1 with the full width at half maximum (FWHM) of 17 cm−1. Compared with the bulk BN, the E2g vibration peak upshifts and the FWHM increases, which is consistent with the reported model of the formation of thin BNNSs decreasing the in-plane lattice constant [32]. Besides, we notice that BNQDs exhibit a much weaker E2g phonon mode at 1378 cm−1 compared with BNNSs, which further indicates the few-layer structure in BNQDs [20]. The FTIR spectra are shown in Fig. 4c. Besides two strong h-BN characteristic peaks at 1380 and 802 cm−1 exist in BNNSs, two weak and broad peaks at 1099 and 652 cm−1 can be also found, corresponding to NBO and OBO bands, respectively, which derives from the oxygenous functional groups and residual B2O3 [33]. In view of BNQDs, the OBO bands still exist and the NBO bands become stronger, confirming the existence of the oxygen on the QDs surface. Meanwhile, the peaks at 1662 cm–1 corresponding to C-(BN) bands can be clearly observed owing to the presence of DMF molecules on the surface of BNQDs [19]. XPS was carried out to further confirm the functional groups on the surface of the BNQDs. As shown in Fig. 4d and e, except for the typical peaks of BN at 190.3 eV and NB at 397.9 eV, the peaks of BO at 191.1 eV and NO/NC at 398.6 eV are also observed [18]. In addition, the existence of the peaks of CN at 285.7 eV and CN/COB
at 288.6 eV further confirms the BNQDs have been functionalized (Fig. 4f), which is in good agreement with FTIR results. The DMF and oxygenous functional groups will be an extremely important character to understand the optical properties and PL.

Fig. 5 a shows the SEM image of h-BN. The morphology of h-BN possesses wrinkle nanosheets with dimensions of up to several micrometers. The typical few-layered sheets are further confirmed by the TEM image. In Fig. 5b, almost transparent nanosheets can be seen, similar to previous reported graphene and exfoliated BNNSs [31]. Besides, HRTEM image (inset of Fig. 5b) shows the lattice spacing is 0.34 nm corresponding to (002) plane of h-BN. Almost all the fringes are (002) planes, which stack and intersect with each other. There are about 4–8 stacked BN layers existing in the nanosheets. Especially, 6 stacked BN layers can be clearly observed at the edge in the magnified inset of Fig. 5b. Sonication treatment is widely used to exfoliate 2D materials to reduce layer thickness and the reduction of the lateral size is usually achieved by solvothermal incision treatment [34]. However, the short time sonication treatment in our case does not significantly alter their microstructures except of the decreasing of the lateral size as shown in Fig. 5c. AFM reveals that the lateral size of BNNSs is not uniform and the thickness is about 1.8–2.7 nm (Fig. 5d), further indicating the as-prepared BNNSs consist of 5–8 layers. Subsequently, the BNNSs are incised into BNQDs by solvents during the solvothermal treatment. As shown in Fig. 5e and f, the average lateral size of resulting BNQDs is about 4.6 nm, which becomes smaller compared to the pristine nanosheet. The HRTEM image shows the lattice spacing is 0.21 nm corresponding to the (100) plane of h-BN (Fig. 5e).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties and PL of BNNSs
ce:section.ce:para: It has been reported that the BNNSs can exhibit nonlinear optical properties and PL in the UV region. However, its visible emission has never been observed. Therefore, UV–vis absorption and PL measurements were carried out for our ultrathin BNNSs before solvothermal treatment. As shown in Fig. 6 a, an adsorption peak at 301.7 nm with a tail expanding to visible region can be seen, which can be attributed to synergetic effect of size and surface defect state [19,35]. Notably, the pristine BNNSs exhibit no PL phenomenon. After sonication, the BNNSs emit strong blue light under the UV-light (365 nm) radiation (Fig. 6a) and the PL QY is 15.4%. The emission is attributed to the surface defects at the edge and on the basal plane upon the ultrasonic treatment in the DMF solution [20]. In addition, the PL of BNNSs can be excited in a wide range of wavelengths from 300 to 400 nm, with a main emission peak at 435 nm and two small shoulders at 410 and 462 nm (Fig. 6b). The peak positions of the PL do not shift with increasing excitation wavelengths, mainly due to the low amount of oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of the BNNSs caused by the short time sonication [36,37]. Meanwhile, we deduce the shoulder peaks may be related to the broad size distribution as illustrated in Fig. 5c. As we all know, the strong DMF solvents usually functionalize BNNSs to form a system of complexes during the sonication exfoliation [38]. Due to the high density defects in the few-layered structure of the BNNSs as confirmed by XRD and TEM, the thin BNNSs are more easier to be attacked and functionalized by DMF to form surface states serving as capture centers of carries, which causes the surface state related PL [39].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties and PL of BNQDs
ce:section.ce:para:
To obtain BNQDs, the ultrasonicate sample was further treated by solvothermal incision. Fig. 7 shows the UV–vis absorption, PL and PL excitation (PLE) spectra of the BNQDs. As shown in Fig. 7a and b, an adsorption peak at 302 nm (4.1 eV) and two excited peaks at 320 nm (3.87 eV) and 394 nm (3.14 eV) can be found, similar to the previously reported BNQDs [18]. The PL spectrum exhibits a peak at 474 nm (2.62 eV) with a FWHM of 50 nm (Fig. 7b). The Stokes shift is 1.48 eV, which is too large for a direct excitonic recombination [40,41]. This indicates the existence of an unconventional in-gap state, most likely corresponding to the defect-related states due to the oxygenous functional groups on the surface of the BNQDs. Compared with BNNSs before solvothermal treatment, the shoulder peaks disappear with the main peak red-shift from 435 to 474 nm and the color of the BNQDs solution changes from strong blue to blue-green under UV-light, as illustrated in Fig. 7c. In addition, with increase of the excitation wavelength, the emission peaks of the BNQDs shift to long wavelength ranging from 420 to 550 nm (Fig. 7d). The excitation-dependent PL behavior is similar to that of the BNQDs synthesized in DMF solvent with strong polarity, mostly resulting from the formation of more oxygenous functional groups on the surface of BNQDs during the solvothermal treatment [20]. The formation of the QDs with uniform size results in the quenching of the shoulder bands in the PL spectra of BNNSs and the different content of the oxygenous functional groups leads to the red shift of the main peak [42]. Fig. 7e shows the PL decay curve, the curve is fitted by a bi-exponential function of I
t
=
A 1
exp
-
t
τ 1
+
A 2
e x p ( -
t
τ 2
) with two time components (τ1 and τ2) and weights (A1 and A2) [43]. The amplitude-weighted average lifetime is evaluated by the following formula:
(1)
τ
a v
=
A 1
τ 1
2
+
A 2
τ 2
2
A 1
τ 1
+
A 2
τ 2

The fast decay component of 2.59 ns with the weight of 49.2% and the slow decay component of 9.13 ns with the weight of 50.8% are obtained. As a result, the average lifetime is calculated to be 7.7 ns, which is in nanosecond, suggesting that the BNQDs are promising candidate materials for optoelectronic and biological applications.

Fig. 7f displays the time dependence of the PL intensity of as-prepared BNQDs, which was recorded by a Xenon lamp at an excitation wavelength of 394 nm. As observed in Fig. 7f, the PL intensity of BNQDs has undetectable change. Even after 1.8 h of irradiation, it maintains 98% of its initial value, which is more stable than the reported CdSe-derived core/shell gradient alloy QDs, MoS2/WS2 QDs, and CDs [34,44,45]. Besides, samples stored in the original formation solution still show strong emission after storage for half one year, revealing that the as-prepared BNQDs have excellent photo and chemical stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Temperature-dependent PL and application as the temperature sensor
ce:section.ce:para:
In order to investigate the nonradiative relaxation processes in QDs, the temperature dependence of the normalized PL intensity was analyzed. The temperature-dependent PL was studied by immobilized BNQDs on a freshly SiO2 matrix to form a film as shown in inset of Fig. 8 b and the resulting film also displays strong fluorescence. The temperature was tested by the sensing measurement based on BNQDs as described in Fig. 10. Fig. 8a shows that the PL intensities of the BNQDs strongly depend on the temperature, and decrease almost linearly with the increase of temperature from 80 to 440 K when excited at 394 nm (Fig. 5b). With the temperature increasing, the nonradiative relaxation process becomes thermally activated, resulting in a decrease in the PL intensity. The solid line is the fitted curve using the Arrhenius equation as following [46]
(2)
I
T
=
I 0
1 + A e x p ( -
E a
K B
T
)
Where I (T) is the PL intensity at temperature T, I0 is the PL intensity at 0 K, A is a parameter related to radiative lifetime. As shown in Fig. 8c, the PL intensity is thermally activated with an activation energy Ea of 83.9 meV, which is close to the g-C3N4 [23]. Furthermore, we notice that FHWM exhibits a slight change with temperature, revealing the FHWM is basically temperature independent (Fig. 8d). The total line width can be explained by multi factors of inhomogeneity broadening and homogeneous broadening including phonon-exciton and acoustic interactions [41].
(3)
Γ
T
=
Γ
i n h
+ σ T +
Γ
L O
exp
E
L O
k B
T
- 1
- 1
Where Γinh is the inhomogeneous line width of the QDs, which is temperature-independent. σ is the electron-acoustic phonon coupling coefficient, ΓLO is the strength of exciton-LO phonon coupling and ELO is the LO-phonon energy. Based on the above equation, the very small variation of FHWM with the temperature indicates that phonon coupling is very weak in the BNQDs. From Fig. 8e, it can be observed that the peak energy also exhibits a negligible change with the temperature, which means electron − electron scattering dominates in BNQDs [47]. The phenomenon is quite different from that of other QDs [40,41]. Trapalis A. et al. observed a similar band and concluded that it is related to deep-level defects [48]. Meanwhile, the temperature-independent chromaticity makes them as a class of materials for potential application as cold-light light emitting diodes (LEDs), QDs displays and temperature sensors [49].

Above results show that the PL intensities decrease linearly with the increase of temperature from 80 to 440 K, which revealing there is a good correspondence between the PL intensity and the environment temperature. Therefore, we can obtain the environment temperature from the PL information. Also, a portable noncontact temperature sensor based on BNQDs can be realized. The effect of the mean size of QDs on the sensitivity of sensors is also discussed. The BNQDs with mean size of about 17.73 nm were synthesized using the BNNSs obtained at 600 ℃as precursor (Fig. 9
a–d). For comparison, we have synthesized BNQDs with mean size of about 4.6 nm using the BNNSs obtained at 900 ℃ as precursor (Fig. 8). From Figs. 5c and 9 a, it can be seen that the lateral size of BNQDs obtained from BNNSs at 600 ℃ is obviously larger than that of BNQDs obtained from BNNSs at 900 ℃. The absolute sensitivity (S) and the relative sensitivity (Sr) can be obtained by the following equation based on Figs. 8b and 9 c [23],
(4)
S =
d ( I ( T ) / I ( 0 ) )
d T
(5)
S r
= S
I ( 0 )
I ( T )
where I(T) is the intensity at temperature T and I(0) is the intensity at room temperature. The Sr of the sensor based on BNQDs with the mean size of 17.73 nm reaches maximum of 0.45% K−1 at 440 K (Fig. 9d), which is lower than the maximum (0.73% K−1 at 440 K) of BNQDs with the mean size of 4.6 nm (Fig. 5c). It should be pointed out that other factors such as the composition and morphology may also attribute to the difference, which will be studied in the future. Moreover, the Sr of BNQDs with the mean size of 4.6 nm is higher than the materials reported in the literature such as ZnO:Er3+ (0.62 % K−1 at 443 K) [50], Mn2+ doped CdS–ZnS core–shell QDs (0.5 % K−1 at 293 K) [51] and Gd2O3:Yb3+/Er3+ UCNPs (0.2 % K−1 at 600 K) [52].

The PL reversibility of sensors based on BNQDs with the mean size of 4.6 nm was investigated by measuring their emission intensities at two temperatures of 293 and 373 K for 10 consecutive cycles. As shown in Fig. 8g, the PL intensities alter reversibly between the two temperatures and there is little change in the PL intensities at each cycle. Table 1 shows a comparison of the present results with previous reported PL probes for noncontact temperature sensors. The current BNQDs exhibit the widest temperature detection range, which covers the entire range of physiological temperature (293∼353 K) [2]. At the same time, the BNQDs still exhibit excellent photostability and good reversibility, which renders the as-prepared BNQDs great potential in the fabrication of temperature sensor.

In summary, we have demonstrated an improved top-down approach for BNQDs preparation, namely, BNNSs with few-layer structure were firstly synthesized and then used to form BNQDs by a short time sonication and a subsequent solvothermal process. The optical properties of BNNSs and BNQDs are systematically investigated. The results show that the oxygenous functional groups on the surface of BNQDs have a strong effect on their PL characteristic. The obtained BNQDs exhibit good photo and chemical stability. Besides, the temperature-dependent PL of BNQDs was studied to understand their PL mechanism and evaluate the possibility of the temperature sensor application. The activation energy of 83.9 meV combined with the good reversibility of the PL intensities means that the as-prepared BNQDs are well-suited for wide-range low temperature sensors. This work verifies that these novel BNQDs could be promising candidates for optical thermometry.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation for Excellent Young Scholars of China (Grant No. 51522402), National Postdoctoral Program for Innovative Talents (BX20180034), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities of NO. FRF-TP-18-045A1 and China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (Grant No. 2018M641192).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Boosting the sensitivity of Nd3+-based luminescent nanothermometers

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Balabhadra, M.L. Debasu, C.D. Brites, L.A. Nunes, O.L. Malta, J. Rocha, M. Bettinelli, L.D. Carlos, Boosting the sensitivity of Nd3+-based luminescent nanothermometers, Nanoscale, 7 (2015) 17261-17267.
J. Rocha, C.D.S Brites, L.D. Carlos. Lanthanide organic framework luminescent thermometers. Chem. Eur. J. 22 (2016): 14782-14795.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nanometre-scale thermometry in a living cell

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G. Kucsko, P.C. Maurer, N.Y. Yao, M. Kubo, H.J. Noh, P.K. Lo, H. Park, M.D. Lukin, Nanometre-scale thermometry in a living cell, Nature, 500 (2013) 54-58.
Y. Yue, X. Wang, Nanoscale thermal probing, Nano Rev. 3 (2012).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A hydrothermal route to water-stable luminescent carbon dots as nanosensors for pH and temperature

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Wang, Z. Xu, H. Cheng, H. Lin, M.G. Humphrey, C. Zhang, A hydrothermal route to water-stable luminescent carbon dots as nanosensors for pH and temperature, Carbon, 82 (2015) 87-95.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Luminescent probes and sensors for temperature

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X.D. Wang, O.S. Wolfbeis, R.J. Meier, Luminescent probes and sensors for temperature, Chem. Soc. Rev, 42 (2013) 7834-7869.
C.D. Brites, P.P. Lima, N.J. Silva, A. Millan, V.S. Amaral, F. Palacio, L.D. Carlos, Thermometry at the nanoscale, Nanoscale, 4 (2012) 4799-4829.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A temperature sensor based on CdTe quantum dots-layered double hydroxide ultrathin films via layer-by-layer assembly

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Q. Li received the B. Eng. Degree in the department of Metallurgy and Energy in North China University of Science and Technology in 2014. Her research focuses on preparation of materials, electrochemical and adsorption application. She is currently studying for his PhD degree in the school of Metallurgical and Ecological engineering, University of Science and Technology Beijing, China.

X. M. Hou received her bachelor's and master's degree in Zhengzhou University, and got her PhD degree in University of Science and Technology Beijing in 2009. Now she is a full professor of University of Science and Technology Beijing. Her research interests include the preparation of high performance ceramics, high temperature properties and functional applications in such fields of electrochemistry and environment.
